answer:
Iqbal engages **warmly** with Hindi figures and motifs—often in a spirit of shared cultural heritage, spiritual admiration, or national pride—in several poems across *Bang-e-Dara*. He directly addresses a Brahman as a fellow seeker, elevates Ram as a spiritual exemplar for Hindustan, and eulogizes the Hindu renunciant Swami Ram Tirath as having attained divine unity. Hindustan (Hindostan) appears affectionately as a homeland in natural odes like "Himalaya," where it symbolizes enduring majesty.[^1][^2][^3][^4]

He engages **polemically** when invoking these figures to critique Muslim decadence or disunity, portraying Hindu steadfastness (e.g., the Brahmin's sacred cord) or cultural influence as a foil to Islamic decline. A cleric in one poem attacks Iqbal himself for allegedly deeming "no Hindu infidel," framing such tolerance as suspect. Iqbal rebukes Muslims for adopting a "Hindu" culture, equating it with dilution of faith.[^5][^6][^7]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 36 ("Sach keh doon ae Brahman! gar tu bura na maane"), translation, [p-752d60](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/36-sach-keh-doon-ae-brahman-gar-tu-bura-na-maane/translation/#p-752d60).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 17 ("Ram"), translation, [p-f8aace](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/17-ram/translation/#p-f8aace).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 23 ("Swami Ram Tirath"), translation ([full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/)).
[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 25 ("Himalaya"), translation, [p-b8c905](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/25-himalaya/translation/#p-b8c905).
[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4 ("Dil se jo baat nikalti hai asar rakhti hai"), translation, [p-172a4f](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-172a4f).
[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 44 ("Zuhd aur Rindi"), translation, [p-266324](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/44-zuhd-aur-rindi/translation/#p-266324).
[^7]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 8 ("Ghurra-e-"), translation, [p-67039c](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/08-ghurra-e/translation/#p-67039c).
